{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: - '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' - '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) - '''Kirishima City Designation:''' - '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese) {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:qq]] In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: - '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' - '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) - '''Kirishima City Designation:''' - '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese) {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:qq]] revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: - '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' - '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) - '''Kirishima City Designation:''' - '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: - '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' - '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) - '''Kirishima City Designation:''' - '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: - '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' - '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) - '''Kirishima City Designation:''' - '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese) {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:qq]] revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: - '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' - '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) - '''Kirishima City Designation:''' - '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese) {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyaura revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: - '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' - '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) - '''Kirishima City Designation:''' - '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese) {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyaura revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: - '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' - '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) - '''Kirishima City Designation:''' - '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese) {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyaura revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: - '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' - '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) - '''Kirishima City Designation:''' - '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese) {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyaura revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: - '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' - '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) - '''Kirishima City Designation:''' - '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese) {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyaura revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: - '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' - '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) - '''Kirishima City Designation:''' - '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese) {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyaura revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: * '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' * '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) * '''Kirishima City Designation:''' * '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese) {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyaura revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: * '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' ** '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) * '''Kirishima City Designation:''' ** '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese) {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyaura {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: * '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' ** '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) * '''Kirishima City Designation:''' ** '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: * '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' ** '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) * '''Kirishima City Designation:''' ** '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese) {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyaura revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG|250px]]
Photographed in December 2013 | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = }} '''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima|ja|霧島市}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|ja|大隅半島}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|ja|鹿児島湾}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|ja|桜島}} across the bay, making it a scenic location. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities. - The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代}} include {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊}}, and {{ill|Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊}}. == History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|ja|大隅国}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|ja|熊襲}} tribe. The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣}}. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}. == Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos. == Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion. == Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|天然記念物}}. The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone." == Cultural Properties == Categories and designation dates in parentheses: * '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' ** '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) * '''Kirishima City Designation:''' ** '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986) == References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}} == External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese) {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyaura Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyaura
