{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima, Kagoshima|en|Kirishima, Kagoshima|WD=Q858352|lt=Kirishima|1=Kirishima, Kagoshima}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神世七代|1=Kamiyonanayo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275893}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Generations of Earthly Deities|1=Five Generations of Earth Deities|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18337308}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Kirishima|WD=Q858352|1=Kirishima, Kagoshima}} | coordinates = {{Coord|31.671083|N|130.819528|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima, Kagoshima|en|Kirishima, Kagoshima|WD=Q858352|lt=Kirishima|1=Kirishima, Kagoshima}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|en|Ōsumi Province|WD=Q981002|lt=Ōsumi Province|1=Ōsumi Province}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.
The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|en|Ōsumi Peninsula|WD=Q386078|lt=Ōsumi Peninsula|1=Ōsumi Peninsula}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|en|Kagoshima Bay|WD=Q1141442|lt=Kagoshima Bay|1=Kagoshima Bay}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|en|Sakurajima|WD=Q128841|lt=Sakurajima|1=Sakurajima}} across the bay, making it a scenic location.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神世七代|1=Kamiyonanayo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275893}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Generations of Earthly Deities|1=Five Generations of Earth Deities|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18337308}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities.
- The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神世七代|1=Kamiyonanayo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275893}} include {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuninotokotachi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国之常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokumununu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨクモノ}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uhijini|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウヒヂニ・スヒヂニ|1=Uhijini and Suhijini|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9090923}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suhijini|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウヒヂニ・スヒヂニ|1=Uhijini and Suhijini|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9090923}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Generations of Earthly Deities|1=Five Generations of Earth Deities|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18337308}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q462555}}, and {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ninigi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|en|Ōsumi Province|WD=Q981002|lt=Ōsumi Province|1=Ōsumi Province}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiko-no-hana|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|en|Kumaso|WD=Q1196897|lt=Kumaso|1=Kumaso}} tribe.
The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimazu Shigehide|1=Shimazu Narinobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8514648}}.
In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|en|Satsuma Rebellion|WD=Q714820|lt=Satsuma Rebellion|1=Satsuma Rebellion}}.
== Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos.
== Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion.
== Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural monument|en|Natural monument|WD=Q23790|lt=Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Natural monument}}.
The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone."
== Cultural Properties == <small>Categories and designation dates in parentheses:</small> * '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' ** '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) * '''Kirishima City Designation:''' ** '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986)
== References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyaura Shrine (宮浦宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Miyaura-Guu Fukuyama Kagoshima 2013.JPG | location = 2437 Fukuyama, Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima, Kagoshima|en|Kirishima, Kagoshima|qid=Q858352|lt=Kirishima|1=Kirishima, Kagoshima}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|qid=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}} | Position = {{ill-coordinates|31|40|15.9|N|130|49|10.3|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神世七代|1=Kamiyonanayo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3275893}}, {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Generations of Earthly Deities|1=Five Generations of Earth Deities|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18337308}} | Classification = Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}, {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} | Established = Unknown | MainHall = Shinmei-zukuri | Alternatename = Miyaura Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage = | AnnualFestival = April 3 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Kirishima|qid=Q858352|1=Kirishima, Kagoshima}} | coordinates = {{Coord|31.671083|N|130.819528|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Miyaura Shrine''' (宮浦宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fukuyama Town, {{ill|Kirishima, Kagoshima|en|Kirishima, Kagoshima|qid=Q858352|lt=Kirishima|1=Kirishima, Kagoshima}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|qid=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}}. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|en|Ōsumi Province|qid=Q981002|lt=Ōsumi Province|1=Ōsumi Province}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}.
The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|en|Ōsumi Peninsula|qid=Q386078|lt=Ōsumi Peninsula|1=Ōsumi Peninsula}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|en|Kagoshima Bay|qid=Q1141442|lt=Kagoshima Bay|1=Kagoshima Bay}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|en|Sakurajima|qid=Q128841|lt=Sakurajima|1=Sakurajima}} across the bay, making it a scenic location.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, the {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神世七代|1=Kamiyonanayo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3275893}} (12 deities), and the {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Generations of Earthly Deities|1=Five Generations of Earth Deities|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18337308}} (5 deities), totaling 18 deities.
- The {{ill|Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja|天神七代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Generations of Heavenly Deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神世七代|1=Kamiyonanayo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3275893}} include {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuninotokotachi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国之常立神}}, {{ill|Toyokumununu|ja|豊雲野神|1=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokumununu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨクモノ}}, and the paired deities starting from {{ill|Uhijini|ja|宇比地邇神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uhijini|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウヒヂニ・スヒヂニ|1=Uhijini and Suhijini|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q9090923}} and {{ill|Suhijini|ja|沙土煮尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suhijini|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウヒヂニ・スヒヂニ|1=Uhijini and Suhijini|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q9090923}}. - The {{ill|Five Generations of Earthly Deities|ja|地神五代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Generations of Earthly Deities|1=Five Generations of Earth Deities|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18337308}} include {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|天忍穂耳尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q462555}}, and {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ninigi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ninigi|ja|天津日高彦火邇々杵尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History == The shrine is identified with the "Miyaura Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} under {{ill|Ōsumi Province|en|Ōsumi Province|qid=Q981002|lt=Ōsumi Province|1=Ōsumi Province}}, but records prior to 1752 (Hōreki 2) are unclear. Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiko-no-hana|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|en|Kumaso|qid=Q1196897|lt=Kumaso|1=Kumaso}} tribe.
The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimazu Shigehide|1=Shimazu Narinobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8514648}}.
In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|en|Satsuma Rebellion|qid=Q714820|lt=Satsuma Rebellion|1=Satsuma Rebellion}}.
== Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos.
== Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion.
== Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural monument|en|Natural monument|qid=Q23790|lt=Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Natural monument}}.
The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone."
== Cultural Properties == <small>Categories and designation dates in parentheses:</small> * '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' ** '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) * '''Kirishima City Designation:''' ** '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986)
== References == * {{Cite book|title=Survey of Shikinaisha Volume 24, Saikaidō|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1978}} * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place names Vol. 46: Kagoshima Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1983}}
== External Links == * [http://www.kagojinjacho.or.jp/search/airaisa/kirishima/post-997.html Miyaura Shrine (Kagoshima Shrine Office)] (Japanese)

It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for {{ill|Ōsumi Province|en|Ōsumi Province|qid=Q981002|lt=Ōsumi Province|1=Ōsumi Province}}, with the former classification as a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}.
The shrine is situated on a flat area of sloped terrain near the western base of the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|en|Ōsumi Peninsula|qid=Q386078|lt=Ōsumi Peninsula|1=Ōsumi Peninsula}}. Overlooking {{ill|Kagoshima Bay|en|Kagoshima Bay|qid=Q1141442|lt=Kagoshima Bay|1=Kagoshima Bay}}, it directly faces {{ill|Sakurajima|en|Sakurajima|qid=Q128841|lt=Sakurajima|1=Sakurajima}} across the bay, making it a scenic location.

Legends associate the area with Emperor Jimmu's youth and refer to the nearby cape, {{ill|Wakamiko-no-hana|ja|若尊鼻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiko-no-hana|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, named for his childhood title. Alternative legends suggest the cape's name originates from {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} during his conquest of the {{ill|Kumaso|en|Kumaso|qid=Q1196897|lt=Kumaso|1=Kumaso}} tribe.
The shrine likely originated as a maritime shrine at a strategic location connecting land routes to the east and sea routes along the western Kagoshima Bay coast. The shrine was severely damaged by eruptions of Sakurajima, with its buildings destroyed in a fire in 1791 (Kansei 3). It was rebuilt by {{ill|Shimazu Shigehide|ja|島津斉宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimazu Shigehide|1=Shimazu Narinobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8514648}}.
In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. However, it sustained damage during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|en|Satsuma Rebellion|qid=Q714820|lt=Satsuma Rebellion|1=Satsuma Rebellion}}.
== Festivals == The shrine’s main festival was historically held on January 25 to commemorate Emperor Jimmu’s eastward expedition. It was later moved to March 10, and now occurs on April 3. Before 1752, weaving and moxibustion were prohibited before the festival due to associated taboos.
== Buildings == The main hall (honden) is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} style, measuring three ken by two ken. The original structure, rebuilt in 1791, was lost during the Satsuma Rebellion.
== Grounds == Two massive ginkgo trees, called the "Married Couple Ginkgo," stand in front of the main hall. Legends attribute their planting to Emperor Jimmu, with both trees estimated to be over 1,000 years old. They are designated {{ill|Natural monument|en|Natural monument|qid=Q23790|lt=Natural Monuments of Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Natural monument}}.
The site also contains five auxiliary shrines and relics such as a stone marker and a rock known as the "Imperial Seat Stone."
== Cultural Properties == <small>Categories and designation dates in parentheses:</small> * '''Kagoshima Prefecture Designation:''' ** '''Fukuyama Ginkgo Trees''' (Natural Monument, June 5, 1964) * '''Kirishima City Designation:''' ** '''Leather Armor''' (Tangible Cultural Property, December 11, 1986)
